coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of N-doped carbon quantum dots from bio-waste lignin for selective irons detection and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Yixin
Liu, Xin
Wang, Meng
Huang, Jianbo
Jiang, Xueqin
Pang, Jinhui
Xu, Feng
Zhang, Xueming
coredata.dc:description: Facile synthesis of carbon quantum dots with high fluorescence and excellent biocompatibility from plentiful and biocompatible materials still attracts much attention because of their great potential value in sensors and imaging. In this study, a highly fluorescent and super biocompatible N-doped carbon quantum dots derived from green precursor of aminated alkali lignin has been synthesized for cellular imaging. The amine-rich lignin carbon quantum dots (AL-CQDs) was firstly synthesized by introduction of secondary amine groups into lignin backbones via both Mannich and Machael addition reaction. The resulted AL-CQDs within the range of 4–10 nm exhibited excitation-dependent and pH-stable fluorescence properties and was employed for the detection of irons with a wide range from 100 nM to 1 mM and a low detection limit of 8 nM, in which the Fe3+ ions could be captured by the amine groups of the AL-CQDs to form an absorbent complex, resulting in a significant fluorescence quenching. Meanwhile, the AL-CQDs were successfully applied to cell imaging and intracellular irons detection benefited from low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. This work would shed much light on converting agricultural waste into bio-based nanomaterials and their potential applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amination
Lignin
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence
Irons detection
Cellular imaging

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Lignin and its blends, Edited by : Dr. Maurice Collins and Dr. Roberto Rinaldi

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of aminated lignin carbon quantum dots (AL-CQDs)
Sensitivity and selective detection of Fe3+ ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful for the financial support of this research from the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2017YFD0601004 ), Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31470606 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of AL-CQDs. (b) AFM image of AL-CQDs.
(a) XRD patterns of AL-CQDs. (b) Raman spectrum of AL-CQDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of lignin and AL-CQDs. (d) XPS survey scan of AL-CQDs. High resolution XPS spectra of (e) C1s and (f) N1s of AL-CQDs.

(a) Photographs of the AL-CQDs aqueous solution excitated under UV light of 365 nm (left) and sunlight (right). (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the AL-CQDs solution. (c) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of AL-CQDs solution. (d) Fluorescence spectra of AL-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 240 nm to 400 nm. (e) Fluorescence spectra of AL-CQDs at various pH values (pH = 1–13). (f) Time-resolved photoluminescent decay and fitting curves of AL-CQDs.

(a) Fluorescence change of AL-CQDs solutions with different Fe3+ concentrations from 0 to 1 mM. (b) AL-CQDs solution and AL-CQDs with Fe3+ solution under 365 nm UV light irradiation. (c) The difference of fluorescence change (F/F0) about AL-CQDs solution in the presence of various metal ions. (d) Fluorescence intensities of AL-CQDs with presence of individual metal ion solutions (black column), and coexistence of Fe3+ ion with other metal ions (red column). (e) The function of fluorescence change (F/F0) of AL-CQDs on the concentration of Fe3+ ions ranging from 0 to 1 mM (insert: The Stern-Volmer plots for the concentration of Fe3+ over the range of 0–50 μM). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cell viability of mouse macrophage cells (n = 5, mean ± S.D.) after being treated with various final concentrations of AL-CQDs. (Untreated cells were served as the control, whose viability was set as 100%).

(a–e) Fluorescence images of RAW 264.7 cells incubated AL-CQDs under bright light, excitation wavelengths of 405, 488 and 558 nm for each horizontal channel and the overlay, respectively. (f–j) Fluorescence images of RAW 264.7 cells incubated AL-CQDs with Fe3+ ions under bright light, excitation wavelengths of 405, 488 and 558 nm for each horizontal channel and the overlay, respectively. (Scale bar = 7.5 μm.)
Possible modification mechanism of lignin with ethylene diamine and representation of the preparation route AL-CQDs and its quenching by Fe3+ ions.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of N-doped carbon quantum dots from bio-waste lignin for selective irons detection and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Lignocellulosic Chemistry, 35 Qinghua East Road, Haidian District, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing 100083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Lignocellulosic Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Marine Science and Biological Engineering, 99 Songling Road, Laoshan District, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266042, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Marine Science and Biological Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Facile synthesis of carbon quantum dots with high fluorescence and excellent biocompatibility from plentiful and biocompatible materials still attracts much attention because of their great potential value in sensors and imaging. In this study, a highly fluorescent and super biocompatible N-doped carbon quantum dots derived from green precursor of aminated alkali lignin has been synthesized for cellular imaging. The amine-rich lignin carbon quantum dots (AL-CQDs) was firstly synthesized by introduction of secondary amine groups into lignin backbones via both Mannich and Machael addition reaction. The resulted AL-CQDs within the range of 4–10 nm exhibited excitation-dependent and pH-stable fluorescence properties and was employed for the detection of irons with a wide range from 100 nM to 1 mM and a low detection limit of 8 nM, in which the Fe3+ ions could be captured by the amine groups of the AL-CQDs to form an absorbent complex, resulting in a significant fluorescence quenching. Meanwhile, the AL-CQDs were successfully applied to cell imaging and intracellular irons detection benefited from low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. This work would shed much light on converting agricultural waste into bio-based nanomaterials and their potential applications.

N-doped carbon quantum dots derived from bio-waste lignin could be served as fluorescent probe towards Fe3+ ions as well as exhibited great potential prospect in cellular imaging.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has attracted substantial attention for their distinct fluorescence characteristic [1], optical stability [2,3], low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and their potential applications in metal ions sensor, diagnostic, nanofertilizer [4], bio-imaging [5], and biosensor [6]. Since the original report on CQDs has been published in 2006 [7], there are extensive investigations by many research groups around the world, resulting in significant progress in the structural and mechanism understandings [8], together with the exploration of newly synthetic strategies and potential applications [9–12].

Abundant synthetic methods of CQDs have been developed, in which the synthesis of CQDs could be accomplished through many synthetic approaches such as electrochemistry, electrochemical oxidation treatments [13], carbonization methods, microwave synthesis routes [14], combustion, ultrasonic-assisted routes, hydrothermal/solvent heat treatments [15], acidic oxidation etc. Whereas, many of these synthesis approaches require poisonous or expensive precursors, complex reaction conditions are hard for further-modification [16,17]. Therefore, exploration of new methods and green precursors for synthesizing CQDs is still necessary. Over the past few decades, various precursors were recognized for the synthesis of CQDs, such as citric acid [18,19], phenylenediamine [20], rhodamine [21], ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid [22,23] and thiourea [24]. In addition, a number of researches have carried out the research of synthesis CQDs using natural products as raw materials during the recent years. For instance, gelatins [25], silk [26], apple juice [27], rose-heart radish [28], human hair [29], honey [30], and together with carbohydrates including glycerol [31], chitosan [32–34], cellulose and cyclodextrin [35], and sucrose are typically used [36]. What's better, greener precursors derived from bio-wastes and biomass derivatives could also be used, such as paper ash [37], grass [38], fruit peel [39], leaves [40], castor oil [41] and plant bark [42]. In order to meet the requirement of both environment-friendly and readily available, it is quite important to find suitable starting materials with good biocompatibility and biodegradability. One of such raw materials is lignin, which is an abundant natural organic polymer with sp2-hybridized benzene rings, demonstrating an excellent source of carbon precursors [43,44].

Lignin is the most abundant aromatic bio-based macromolecules, which is a renewable and green raw material supplied by all types of agricultural and forestry plants. Generally, lignin is considered as industrial waste in the pulp and paper industry and directly disposed by combustion process to generate heat [45]. Depending on the sources and pulping processes, different kinds of lignin samples, such as alkaline lignin, lignosulfonate and Kraft lignin with different monomers and functional groups composition could be produced [46,47]. Recently, some researchers have carried out synthesis of nano-materials for drug release [48,49], detection of high-valence metal ions and cellular imaging using different lignin samples, such as cellulolytic enzyme lignin [50], alkali lignin [51] and lignosulfonate lignin [52]. Compared with those conventional CQDs, lignin-based CQDs have exhibited extraordinary superiority, including low cytotoxicity, optical stability, and environmentally-friendly. Despite substantial efforts have achieved, new strategies for fabricating of nitrogen-doped lignin-based carbon dots via chemical means have not been reported. Herein, a facile method for preparation a novel NH2-rich lignin carbon quantum dots via Mannich reaction has been established. Due to the introduction of primary amine groups into lignin backbones, the aminated lignin carbon quantum dots (AL-CQDs) exhibited excellent fluorescence activity, pH-stability and sensibility and were envisioned to apply in highly efficient detection of Fe3+ ions on the basis of the fluorescence quenching. Moreover, the AL-CQDs exhibited excellent biocompatibility in cellular imaging. Therefore, the synthesis of AL-CQDs as fluorescent probes provided a simple, efficient and sustainable, sensitive and selective approach for cellular imaging and detecting metal ions in biomedicine and environment-monitor aspects.

Alkali lignin (hydroxyl value: >3.0%, ash content: <0.5%, water content: <14.0%) was kindly provided by Shandong Longlive Bio-Technology Co, Ltd. Mouse macrophage RAW 246.7 were purchased from Yaji Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The formaldehyde (AR), ethylenediamine (AR), sodium hydroxide (AR), hydrochloric acid (AR), 3-(4, 5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT), fetal bovine serum (FBS), phosphate buffered saline (PBS), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and other chemicals were used without further purification.

The aminated lignin was successfully prepared via Mannich reaction as shown in the following procedures. Firstly, 5 g alkali lignin was dissolved in 1 mol/L NaOH aqueous solution in a three-necked flask and stirred at room temperature for 10 min. And then, ethylene diamine and formaldehyde were added dropwise under stirring for 5 h at 85 °C. After that, the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 5–6 with 1 mol/L HCl solution under stirring at room temperature. When aminated lignin was totally precipitated, the solid product was filtrated and washed thoroughly with distilled water and dried under vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of aminated lignin carbon quantum dots (AL-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the AL-CQDs were prepared through the following procedures including carbonization, ball-milling and collection. Briefly, the aminated lignin was firstly carbonized at 300 °C with a standing time of 2 h under nitrogen atmosphere. Then, the obtained products were ground in planetary ball mill for 24 h. Eventually, the black fine powders were dispersed in deionized water, and the AL-CQDs were collected by filtrating for further characterizations and use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) observations were performed using JEOL JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope. The atomic-force microscopy (AFM) observation was acquired by Bruker MultiMode 8 AFM system. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to investigate the crystalline phase of AL-CQDs by using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE diffractometer. Raman spectrum was recorded on a LabRAM HR Evolution Raman spectrometer using a 532 nm laser beam and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) were recorded by Nicolet iN10 FT-IR spectrometer. The surface composition of AL-CQDs were analysed by ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using Al Kα as the radiation source. UV–vis spectra were recorded using a TECHCOMP UV2300 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectroscopy (FL) was performed using a Hitachi F-7000 spectrofluorometer with a Xe lamp as an excitation source. The quantum yield (QY) was obtained on an Edinburgh FLS980 spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selective detection of Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: For the sensitivity experiment, an appropriate amount of anhydrous ferric chloride (FeCl3) was used to prepare different concentration of Fe3+ ions solution. Certain amount of AL-CQDs solution (2 mL, 0.5 mg/mL) was added to different concentration Fe3+ ions solution (2 mL), and the mixtures were maintained for 10 min at room temperature. The FL was recorded using the excitation wavelength of 280 nm.

In order to compare the sensitivity of AL-CQDs for different ions, fluorescence spectroscopy with presence of other metal ions (K+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Na+, Ba2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Hg2+ and Cr3+ ions) was also detected according to the similar steps depicted above. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of AL-CQDs with the presence of co-existed different metal ions and Fe3+ have also been measured, in which fluorescence spectroscopy of AL-CQDs solution and 1 mM different ion solution with and without Fe3+ ion were recorded using spectrofluorometer.

The prepared AL-CQDs solution (1 mL, 0.5 mg/mL) was mixed with the solution (3 mL) at different pH values (pH = 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, and 13). The mixture solution was maintained for 10 min at room temperature. Then, the fluorescence properties of AL-CQDs at various pH values were evaluated by fluorescence spectroscopy.

The cytotoxicity of AL-CQDs was evaluated by MTT assay against mouse macrophages RAW 264.7 cell line. Firstly, RAW 264.7 cells were kept in Dulbecco's Modified Eagles Medium (DMEM) with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). Then, 100 μL RAW 264.7 cells were seeded at density of 1 × 104 cells per well in 96-well tissue culture plates with DMEM media and incubated for 24 h in a humidified incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 °C. After that, the cells plates were added in the different concentrations gradient of AL-CQDs from 0 to 100 μg/mL for 24 h. Then, the 200 μL of new medium and 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL, 0.5% MTT) were introduced to each well for 4 h. Next, the culture medium was aspirated carefully in the well and terminated the culture. Finally, 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were introduced to each well and shook at low speed on a shaker to dissolve the crystals. The absorbance of sample was evaluated at OD 570 nm with microplate reader (Bio-Rad). Each experiment was repeated three times and each sample used nine replicate wells.

In order to investigate cellular imaging application of AL-CQDs, RAW 264.7 was seeded in a 24-well plate in which glass slides were placed. Cell cultures plate was cultured in an incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, cells were incubated with fresh cell medium containing 10 μg/mL Fe3+ for 1 h. And, a volume of 20 μL AL-CQDs solution (0.5 mg/mL) was added in the cell medium. After that, the cell-grown glass slides were taken out and washed three times with fresh medium and phosphate buffer saline (PBS). Finally, cell imaging was observed by a Leica SP8 confocal microscope.

Lignin, a complex three-dimensional network of phenolic polymers which is composed of phenylpropane units and linked by the elemental units of β-O-4 accounting for the whole linkages more than half in the lignin matrix, and other linkages are β-1, β-5 and β-β [53]. Additionally, lignin also includes various sorts of active groups such as aromatic nucleus, phenolic/alcoholic hydroxyl, carbonyl, methoxy and carboxyl groups which together endow lignin with distinct properties. It has been shown that nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs would enhance its dissolubility in aqueous medium and improve quantum yield (QY) [51]. Therefore, chemical modification of lignin was innovatively carried out to obtain aminated lignin by introduction of secondary amine groups into lignin backbones. In this process, both Mannich and Machael addition reaction were performed using formaldehyde and ethylene diamine as modifying agents, resulting in an addition of secondary amine group in lignin macromolecule backbones. The incorporation of amine group via Mannich reaction and Machael addition as well as preparation of AL-CQDs are shown in Scheme 1 .

Owing to the CQDs is a kind of organic molecular nanocrystal and consists of many small sp2 hybridized carbon nanodomains fixed within a matrix network comprising mainly sp3 hybridized carbon atoms. Therefore, it has been reported that the key to synthesis the CQDs is the existence of polycyclic benzenoid aromatic hydrocarbons [54]. Herein, the lignin was used as material and the prepared AL-CQDs would exhibit superior fluorescence emission efficiency due to the presence of substantial benzene rings with sp2-hybridized carbons in lignin macromolecules. As shown in scheme 1, due to the chemical charges of formaldehyde and amine groups, electrophilic (1, 2-ethanediyldiimino) dimethanol was formed under alkaline conditions. At the same time, the lignin structure was deprotonated, resulting in addition of (1, 2-ethanediyldiimino) dimethanol in the C-5 position of guaiacyl unit because this carbon site possesses high electron density [55]. Moreover, condensation between lignin molecular chain might happen due to the bridging effect of (1, 2-ethanediyldiimino) dimethanol. In addition, the ethylene diamine can directly react with lignin in the presence of air due to the formation of quinoid structure, resulting in the addition of ethylene diamine in benzene ring of lignin polymers. After modification, the AL-CQDs with highly intrinsic emission efficiency was obtained by carbonization of aminated lignin fragments and followed by milling and filtration. Moreover, the significant fluorescence quenching of the AL-CQDs solution appeared with the introduction of Fe3+ ions.

The nanoparticle size distribution was evaluated by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). As shown in Fig. 1(a), the AL-CQDs, with diameter ranged from 4 to 10 nm, were quasi-spherical and uniformly dispersed in aqueous solution. Furthermore, the high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. 1a) clearly displayed more detailed structure that the AL-CQDs possessed crystalline structure with the lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, which was assigned to the presence of the (102) crystal plane of graphite [56].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) images in Fig. 2(a) demonstrated the crystallinity of AL-CQDs and broad XRD peak was observed at the central position (25°), indicating highly disordered carbon atoms in AL-CQDs. In addition, as exhibited in the Raman spectrum of AL-CQDs (Fig. 2b), D band was observed at 1331 cm−1, which represented the degree of disorder of the material structure and a strong G band was found at 1571 cm−1, which was caused by the well-structured graphite single crystal. The ratio of ID/IG, expressing the defect degree of material, was 0.84, indicating that besides sp2 hybridizations there were still some defects or amorphous carbon existing in AL-CQDs. The 2D peak of AL-CQDs could be observed at 2770 cm−1, which demonstrated the formation of graphene-like materials after carbonization [42,57].

The Fourier transform infrared (FI-IR) spectra revealed the molecular structure and chemical composition of lignin and AL-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2(c). The tendency and characteristic peaks of AL-CQDs were basically consistent with that of lignin, indicating that the fundamental skeleton structures were mainly remained after amination and carbonization. The absorption bonds at 3380, 1603 and 1360 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of the OH bond, the vibration of the CC and CO bonds, respectively. Remarkably, the absorption band enhanced substantially at 3200–3400, 2930, 1593, and 1170 cm−1 in the AL-CQDs spectrum, which could be assigned to the NH stretching vibrations and CN bending vibration of quaternary ammonium, separately. Therefore, it could be demonstrated that the Mannich reaction was performed successfully between lignin and (1, 2-ethanediyldiimino) dimethanol. In addition, the introduced functional groups significantly enhanced the solubility and stability of AL-CQDs in solution.

To determine the chemical compositions of the AL-CQDs, the samples were characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2(d), the image displayed distinct peaks at 285, 400 and 532 eV, which corresponded to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The atomic content of the C, O and N elements in the AL-CQDs were evaluated to be 81.58% (C), 13.73% (O), and 4.69% (N), which demonstrated that AL-CQDs contained relatively high contents of nitrogen elements derived from the carbon precursor of aminated lignin. In order to discuss the bond configuration of C and N atoms in AL-CQDs, the high-resolution C1s spectrum is displayed in Fig. 2(e). It was noted that the peaks at 284.6, 285.6, 286.6 and 288.8 eV were assigned to the double bond of carbon (CC), CN bond, hydroxyl groups (C-OH) and carboxylate acid group (OCO), respectively. In addition, the N1s spectrum of AL-CQDs (Fig. 2f) could be fitted into two peaks at 399.3 and 401.2 eV, attributing to the NH bond and CN bond, separately. The XPS spectra also supported that the AL-CQDs were successfully prepared by amination and carbonization process.

The optical absorption of CQDs mainly occurred in the ultraviolet regions, and absorption tails also extended to the visible and near infrared region [58]. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the UV–Vis absorption spectra of AL-CQDs exhibited a distinct absorption peak at 235 nm, which were mainly derived from the electron transition from π → π* of the CC bonds and n → π* of the CO bonds at the edges of AL-CQDs. As compared with the lignin, the AL-CQDs clearly showed a red shift, indicating electron energy level transition due to the introduction of nitrogen atoms in AL-CQDs. Therefore, it could be demonstrated that N-doped groups on the surface of the AL-CQDs slightly adjusted the position of the UV absorption peak, resulting in a polychrome property and being able to detect analytes. Moreover, the fluorescent spectroscopy was also investigated to explore the optical property of the AL-CQDs (Fig. 3c). It was found that the fluorescent spectra of AL-CQDs possessed the maximum emission peak at around 350 nm with the excitation wavelength of 280 nm, and the AL-CQDs exhibited obvious fluorescent emission with excitation at 365 nm as compared with that excited by sunlight (Fig. 3a).

Additionally, different excitation wavelength was used to explore the fluorescent change of AL-CQDs as shown in Fig. 3(d). As the excitation wavelength increased from 240 to 400 nm, the maximum fluorescent peaks of AL-CQDs obviously varied in a wide range from 350 nm to 450 nm. Notably, the blue shift emission peaks were noted and they became stronger as the excitation wavelength increased from 240 to 280 nm. On the contrary, the emission peak exhibited red shift with remarkable decrease of intensity when excitation wavelength enhanced from 280 to 400 nm. These results indicated that the excitation-dependent fluorescent behaviour of AL-CQDs might be attributed to the functional groups on the surface of AL-CQDs [59,60]. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 3(e), the fluorescent spectra of AL-CQDs at various pH systems were also investigated. The AL-CQDs displayed excellent stability in the wide range of pH values from 1 to 13. Apparently, as the pH values increased from 1 to 7, the fluorescent intensity gradually enhanced and reached a maximum value at pH = 7, while that decreased significantly from pH values of 7 to 13. It was demonstrated that the AL-CQDs exhibited excellent stability under the wide range of pH conditions.

Fluorescence lifetime is an intrinsic property of CQDs that correlates with fluorescent sites and the environment. Therefore, the fluorescence decay curve was also calculated by fitting formula to acquire the exponential fitting curve of AL-CQDs (Fig. 3f). The fitting formula is:
(1)
R
t
=
B 1
e
−
t
τ 1
+
B 2
e
−
t
τ 2
+
B 3
e
−
t
τ 3
+
B 4
e
−
t
τ 4
where, B1, B2, B3 and B4 are fractional intensities, τ1, τ2, τ3 and τ4 represent fluorescence lifetime. The related parameters of the photoluminescent decay and instrument response were displayed in the inset of Fig. 3(f). According to formula:
(2)
τ ave
=
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2
+
B 3
τ 3
+
B 4
τ 4
B 1
+
B 2
+
B 3
+
B 4

It was calculated that the τave (average fluorescence lifetime) of AL-CQDs was 5.1993 ns, illustrating that the AL-CQDs was suitable for photoelectricity and biological applications [33]. In addition, the absolute quantum yield (QY) was 8.1%, which was relatively higher as compared with the reported agricultural waste CQDs as shown in Table S1 [33,60].

It was calculated that the τave (average fluorescence lifetime) of AL-CQDs was 5.1993 ns, illustrating that the AL-CQDs was suitable for photoelectricity and biological applications [33]. In addition, the absolute quantum yield (QY) was 8.1%, which was relatively higher as compared with the reported agricultural waste CQDs as shown in Table S1 [33,60].

The response of fluorescence of AL-CQDs for sensing Fe3+ was investigated as shown in Fig. 4(a). It was noted that the fluorescence intensity of AL-CQDs declined gradually as the concentration of Fe3+ increased from 100 nM to 1 mM, indicating that fluorescence quenching occurred due to the presence of Fe3+ ions. The distinct fluorescence quenching of AL-CQDs could be also observed in Fig. 4(b), in which total quenching effect on the fluorescence of AL-CQDs happened with the introduction of Fe3+ ions. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was presumably caused by the formation of aggregates between AL-CQDs and Fe3+ ions. As Fe3+ ions were added into AL-CQDs aqueous solution, the AL-CQDs/Fe3+ aggregates might be formed due to electrostatic adsorption or chelation effect, and charge transfer happened between AL-CQDs and Fe3+ ions, resulting in quenching effect of AL-CQDs [51].

For the sake of exploring the fluorescent sensing of the AL-CQDs in biological and environmental aspects, the fluorescence quenching of AL-CQDs was evaluated by detecting different metal ions (1 mM), including K+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Na+, Ba2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Hg2+ and Cr3+ ions. As observed in Fig. 4(c), the AL-CQDs showed significant fluorescence quenching with the presence of Fe3+ ions, while the addition of other metal ions exhibited a slight and negligible quenching effect. Moreover, it was also noted from Fig. 4(d) that the fluorescence intensities of AL-CQDs (red column) were decreased dramatically due to the introduction of Fe3+ ions solution as compared with addition of individual metal ions (black column). These observations reflected that the AL-CQDs displayed higher selectivity towards Fe3+ ions towards other coexisting metal ions, which might be attributed to the preferable recognition to Fe3+ ions of amine groups on the surface of AL-CQDs [61]. Based on this observation, the as-prepared AL-CQDs could be used for assaying Fe3+ ions without the need to separate Fe3+ ions from the mixtures with other metal ions. Fig. 4(e) exhibited the function of the fluorescence change (F/F0) of AL-CQDs with the concentration of Fe3+ ions. Fluorescence quenching occurred in a wide range from 100 nM to 1 mM and displayed a good linear response, which could be used for quantitative detection of Fe3+ ions.

Besides, the linear relation displayed in Fig. 4(e) inset was evaluated by the Stern-Volmer equation:
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
C
here F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of the system in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions, respectively. Ksv is fluorescence enhancement constant, which is related to the quenching efficiency, and [C] is the concentration of Fe3+ ions. Ksv was calculated to be 3.1 × 10−3 by matching the curve into equation discussed above and the correlation coefficient (R2) was evaluated to be 0.9977. Therefore, the detection limit (3ϭ/slope) for Fe3+ ions was 8 nM. These results showed that the AL-CQDs exhibited superior sensitivity for detection Fe3+ ions and they exhibited great potential in environmental and biological applications.

Equipped with a serious of fascinating features including nanoscale size, fluorescence properties and biocompatibility, CQDs have a great potential in bio-sensing and bio-imaging applications. In order to evaluate the cell viability of AL-CQDs, MTT assay was performed in RAW 246.7 mouse macrophage cell lines. As shown in Fig. 5 , the cell viability had almost not been affected by gradual increasing AL-CQDs concentration. As the AL-CQDs reached 100 μg/mL, the cell viability still remained 96.8%, indicating that AL-CQDs exhibited very low cytotoxicity.

In order to investigate the feasibility of application of AL-CQDs in bio-imaging field, mouse macrophage cell line (RAW 264.7) was used to evaluate the cytocompatibility and bio-imaging capability with AL-CQDs. The AL-CQDs solution were introduced into above mouse macrophage cells and then evaluated by using a laser scanning confocal microscope. As observed from Figs. 6(b)–(d), the cells emitted strong blue (Fig. 6b), green (Fig. 6c) and red fluorescence (Fig. 6d) as excited with wavelengths of 405, 488 and 558 nm, respectively. Therefore, it could be demonstrated that most of AL-CQDs could penetrate into the cytoplasm through the cell membrane and the fluorescent substances appeared widely in the cytoplasmic regions of the cells. However, the nucleus regions showed weak fluorescence because it was difficult to pass through the nuclear membrane. In comparison, when the cells were pretreated with Fe3+ solution, the fluorescence quenching was recorded in Fig. 6(f–j), which demonstrated that the Fe3+ ions in cells could cause fluorescence quenching of AL-CQDs and these results were consistent with earlier research on cells and nanomaterials [62,63]. Therefore, due to the strong and stable fluorescence efficiency, high sensitive for Fe3+ ions detection and biocompatibility, the AL-CQDs could be potentially served as an excellent fluorescence probe for bio-sensing and bio-imaging fields.

To sum up, derived from aromatic lignin macromolecules, the N-doped lignin-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with strong and stable fluorescence have been synthesized via Mannich reaction. The AL-CQDs exhibited the excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence characteristics and the fluorescence intensity of the AL-CQDs was very sensitive to the presence of Fe3+ ions in the system, indicating the high selectivity for the Fe3+ ion detection. Moreover, fluorescence imaging experiment demonstrated that the AL-CQDs could be efficiently uptaken by cells, and showed excellent biocompatibility and images quality. As a nano-fluorescent probe, AL-CQDs could be prepared by a simple and efficient approach, and recognize the target sensitively and selectively, which opens up a new way for the synthesis of nanomaterials derived from agricultural waste.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Table S1
Comparison of previously study about carbon quantum dots derived from bio-wastes precursors.
Table S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2019.01.146.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support of this research from the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFD0601004), Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31470606).
